title: Felix Morgan -Hiring Manager Interview - Andi | Technical Managing Editor
meeting_title: Felix Morgan -Hiring Manager Interview - Andi | Technical Managing Editor

transcript.text: Hi, Felix.
How are you?
Oh, I'm well.
How are you?
I'm good.
I'm so sorry I missed you on Monday.
Oh, that's okay.
I figure you probably have a lot going on.
I, like, was so sure that the call started at 2.30, and then, so I, like, went and then, like, came back, and I was, like, oh, no.
Like, I, like, went to go get myself something to eat, and I was, like, Andi?
I was, like, oh, no.
Thank you for being willing to reschedule.
I'm really sorry I stood you up.
I promise we are not like that normally.
No worries at all.
I didn't, I didn't think anything of it other than just probably logistic stuff, yeah.
Well, your background is really cool.
Where are you located?
This is my home office.
I'm in Austin, Texas.
This is...
This is my canine assistant, Maverick.
He's also an experienced remote worker.
I know that I'm assistant myself.
Oh, nice.
I just saw the shadow.
Yeah.
She's, you know, overseeing.
I've always told Maverick it's time to go to work, and he's like, great, can't wait, and immediately goes to sleep.
So that's perfect.
exactly.
Cool.
Well, I can give you a little bit of background on myself.
I'm sure Marilia gave you some information about the role, but I can talk to you about how I'm thinking about it in the context of GrowthX, and then we can talk about you.
And I try to leave, like, five to ten minutes at the end for you to ask me questions.
Perfect.
Cool.
So I lead customer operations at GrowthX.
That basically means that I'm thinking about, like, process optimization.
Do we have the right people

People in the right roles, do we, like, how are tools operating, all of the pieces that go into making sure that we can efficiently get our deliverables out the door and meet our client's scope of work.
So this role is part of our delivery organization, which is basically what we call the client-facing org, and the team works in a pod configuration.

So when we sign a client, we have an eight-week, we call it strategy sprint, but it's essentially just implementation, right-sizing the, you know, right-sizing the effort, making sure that, like, we have the right stakeholders in there, we have access to their publishing system, all the pieces that we kind of need to know that long-term we're going to have success with a client.
After the eight-week cycle, we go into the eight pod formations, and an ME I think of as kind of like the core of a pod.
So we have

We account strategists and account directors that kind of sit over multiple pods, and they're thinking, like, okay, a client comes out of strategy sprint, this is their plan, these are their goals, this is their plan for the next, like, three to six months.
Um, how are we, you know, what are the KPIs that we're working towards?
How do we, like, kind of keep the, like, and conversation moving?
Like, really a lot of, like, account management and high-level, um, building, thinking about renewals and expansions, things like that.
Um, the ME is really, like, the day-to-day project manager for deliverables.

Um, so they are involved in client calls, um, they're the editor of all the content that goes out, um, and they're kind of making sure that on a week-to-week basis, we are doing what we said we were going to do when we gave them, like, a long-term plan.
Um, so an ME is managing...
Three to five accounts, and I think it'll stay that way for a while, probably aiming for five.
And they work with one to two content managers, CMS, who are running the AI workflows that actually generate our content.
So we have an in-house platform that we use to generate all the content.
It kind of pulls from a bunch of different LLMs, depending on what it's doing, and the goal of the workflow.
And the CMS are running those workflows, generating, like, first draft, making sure that, you know, kind of doing a first pass at editing.
And then the, the ME is thinking, um, okay, does this really align with the brand voice and what I know about the client?
And, um, they're, so they're editing, they're project managing, and that doesn't

Like client facing conversations, some client facing work, and then on the other side, you know, as they're working with the content managers, they're also thinking like, is this workflow doing what I need it to do, and kind of part of their really core part of the feedback loop in terms of how we're leveraging the AI for each particular client.

So we have templated workflows, every client, like, you can think of them as like books on a shelf, every client, when we sign a client, we kind of pull that book off the shelf, customize it for the, you know, for what they need, we they get like a unique workflow.
And then, you know, we need to tweak it depending on, do we need to pull more from their, you know, do we need to pull from like sales calls, or do we need to like get more information here or there.
So thinking about optimizing that because the platform is.
Something that we're building kind of like alongside with building out our services industry.
So that's how the role fits within GrowthX.
And we are, you know, we are at our heart a services agency.
But like I said, we're building out this platform on the other side, which may eventually also be a product offering.
So it's kind of exciting that there's like two things going on.
But that also means you have to like balance two things at once.
Any questions?
Yeah, how many like pods and Emmys do you currently like have?
Like what is that?
What are those teams looking like?
I think we have 11 Emmys.
have two that are joining in the next two weeks.
Yeah.
And then, yeah, we have, and then the content managers, as I said, there's one to two per pods.
have 20 something content managers.
Yeah.
And then
And we're, you know, we're still hiring MEs.
So we're on board between one and three clients every week that are going through Strategy Sprint.
So we're, like, trying to make sure that nobody gets totally overloaded because, like, things feel manageable.
And then it's like, oh, God, we have three clients that are coming out of Sprint.
What are we going to do?
Yeah, that's really cool.
What is the, like, customer, like, like, like I saw a little bit on the website and stuff.
Like, is it a lot of tech?
Is it, like, any other industries that are interested?
I can imagine that this would be so universal, like, an offering.
So I'm just curious what that looks like.
Yeah, I mean, it's a lot of tech across different industries.
So, like, we have, like, EdTech.
We have, like, there's a platform that is made specifically for architects.
We have something that's, like, for manufacturing.
And it is all, like, software.
But the industry is there.
That they serve are all over the place.
Makes sense.
Interesting.
So cool.
Yeah.
Cool.
Well, I would love to learn a little bit more about you.
Yeah, yeah, for sure.
Well, I mean, I'm sure everyone would say this in a job interview, but I'm just like, wow, I'm the perfect person with this role.
So I came up like over the last almost decade as a copywriter first and then content strategy, but I've also done brand and creative work almost exclusively in like B2B SaaS.
companies internally, but I do have experience working for agencies and in other sectors like healthcare and real estate.
And yeah, I just, I'm a bit of a nerd always, like a sweet-spin engineer.
So like, I'm a really creative person, like in my heart, but I have this very like nerdy tech side, which has just served me well in this industry in general.
But specifically with AI, like I'm a big AI nerd.
I read all the books.
I listen to all the podcasts.
I try all the new tools.
And so.
Like, I'm just kind of surprised that, like, adoption with AI hasn't, like, gone faster in the content spaces.
And then also just, like, as a person who's just been writing and editing for so long, I've just been part of a reduction in force at Miro last year.
And since then, I've been kind of doing freelance and really kind of doing a very small version of what it sounds like you guys are doing.

Making AI content processes, like, teaching people, like, how, like, to make AI work and, like, creating, like, duplicatable processes and, like, scale so that people can really up-level their content.
And I feel like, yeah, like, that's just, it's really exciting to me to be on the edge of that.
Like, I don't really feel, like, obviously, I know that this is, like, a turning point for my particular career and, like, the industry as a whole.
But I'm kind of on the, like, positive side of it.
But at the same time, I am, like, interested in finding, like, another full-time position.
And I see the, like, you know, the lack of those or, like, I'm, like, just wondering where we're going next.
So I've been just really thinking about that.
And I've had some success with like the freelance clients, like everyone needs some of that.
But it does just feel like really uniquely like a mix of things I'm really interested in learning and things I'm already doing and experimenting with.
And I have experience like managing writers and designers or agencies as part of internal teams.
I have experience like with client phasing stuff.
I'm one of those like kind of annoying extroverted people.
Like I really just love talking to people.
So I'm not mad about like meetings in the same way.
My boyfriend doesn't get it.
He's like, you just love talking to people.
I'm like, there's nothing I would rather do than like find out this barista's like life story right now.
So yeah, I really enjoy interacting with clients and stuff like that.
This does sound like, I mean, I have more questions and stuff, but like, I like editing.
I like writing.
I love AI.
And like, yeah, so it sounds like a really exciting combination.
Yeah, so tell me, like, what are you doing for your clients right now?
You know, like there's a lot of different ways you can approach.

How freelance, or how are you, how are you, yeah, I mean, hard, some of it is just like, I'm just writing and being transparent about the fact that I'm using AI, like my own AI workflows, and just being transparent about like, that, like some of them are like, we can only use Google and Gemini and some of, you know, like stuff like that, but I'm just like, I can work so much faster, I can make sure that it's really good, and sometimes they're skeptical, and sometimes they're all about it, but, you know, the proof is always kind of in the work.

And then sometimes they really want, they're like, please help me understand how to use this myself, in which case I sometimes like train myself out of a job, right, like where I like, I kind of set up the like things I teach them about, like prompting, but more just about like, it feels like it's less about prompting and more about like context windows, right, like putting all of the good stuff in there, what, what it's going to need to like output this stuff, because it's like, I'm like, it's already pretty good at writing, it's just like, you really have to give it the right materials to be able to like, pull out the right stuff.
So I've done that for a
And then they're like, great, don't really need you anymore.
And I'm like, yeah, I didn't 100% think that through in terms of like a solo business model.
But yeah, that's kind of the two things I've been doing and people are interested in different levels of that.
Yeah.
What has like surprised you the most is the more that you've worked with AI and content generation and like how have you changed your processes as a result?

Oh gosh, like I've been, I've been like messing around with it since the beginning, like before it had a memory and every single time you had to be like, hello, this is what we're working on, you know?
And like, I would like take big projects, make them into PDFs, re-upload the PDFs and all of that.
Now that the memory is better, I honestly think what surprises me the most is that it's not spreading faster.
Like that we're not seeing the adoption, like even in tech where people I think are used to new tools and like big stuff, we're not seeing like the adoption go as fast as the capabilities go.
So, so,
Like, there's so many CMOs who are like, it's just not that good at writing.
You know what I mean?
And I'm like, no, it really is.
Like, it's just interesting to me, you know, that nobody, that, like, people give up kind of quickly on it.
Yeah.
They're like, oh, I didn't do what I wanted right away.
And so, I don't know.
I think that's really surprising.
And what kind of, like, workflows have you built?
What are you using?
Yeah.
Yeah, so I use ChatGDP a lot, Gemini a lot, Claude sometimes, but I'm not really working as much, like, with coding and stuff.
And, like, I find for writing, like, Claude and the other ones kind of seem about the same, at least for what I'm doing.
It's mostly just, like, it's mostly just, like, really, like, volume, right?
So it's, like, instead of doing two blogs, it's, like, 50 blogs.
Or, like, you know, I've worked with a client who needed to, like, basically relaunch their entire website.
So it's just, like, every landing page needed to be touched a little bit.
Like, some of them needed to be net new, but a lot of them just needed, like, consistency and stuff.
So being able to just, like, you know, be like this.
The new messaging, they had also renamed all their products.
It was just like one of those like things that would just take a long time.
Yeah.
That I was able to just be like, here's the page, here's the page, here's the page.
You know, get those to product marketing and get them published.
As they got closer to the launch, they actually were like, Felix, we trust you.
Here's direct access to the WordPress.
And I just went and I just started uploading.
They're like, just do it.
We'll fix it later.
yeah, so.
Yeah.
Cool.
Well, can you talk to me a little bit about your, you know, a time when you had to take a project like end to end and who you worked with, kind of what your process was there?
For sure.
At Miro, I was on kind of, they had like divided their content teams among a number of teams, which was a little bit unusual.
And so I was on the corporate content team and we're really in charge of thought leadership.
But I have experience with like so much full funnel stuff.
So they really wanted to do like specifically target.

So I actually just kind of reached out on my own, extroverted me again, and got together with both product marketing and specifically DemandGen, who was just really like, we want to build more of a funnel, we're always like, you know, we just really don't have this stuff.
So I managed to use, like my team did a lot of like research, like independent research.
So we started there, but then I partnered with the other teams.

So we made like, we took that research report, and then I made like two webinars with like the field marketing team, and then some infographics for the DemandGen team, which I like worked with the brand team to design.
And so like, we really like built this mini funnel specifically for agile, like clients, which hadn't existed before.
And it was super successful.
And even though I was part of like, the layoffs, obviously, it's funny, because my friend was just texting me that it's still being used today.
And people are like, this is what we should be doing.
And I was like, I guess that's kind
Nice to hear that it was still working.

So, yeah, I kind of took that from, like, goal all the way through and just really tried to meet all the needs of the different departments who are all obviously working towards common goals, but sometimes in different ways.
Yeah.
What are you, like, looking for next?
What are you excited about?
mean, you have a freelance, you, like, freelancing.
Yeah, I mean, yeah, freelancing is doing fine.
It's a lot of, like, hustle, right?
Like, it's a lot of, like, scrappiness.
I like kind of more dependable, kind of, like, stable things.
So I think I'd prefer a full-time position in that way.
But I just do feel like there is this big intersection, right?
Like, I'm like, I know that AI can do the writing part of my job, like, as good as me.
I don't know when everyone else is going to figure out how to do it like that, but, like, I know that it can.
And so for me, I'm just really, like, it's either, you know, work for, like, a small company and be like, I can run your whole company.
Content department, just me and the robots, or like, you know, try to get into an AI company, which is really my preference.

Like, as this all gets, like, redefined, I would really rather be part of a company that's, like, redefining some of the stuff, that's, like, figuring it out, that's not going to, like, ignore it until it gets too big or, you know, something like that.
yeah.
I want to, like, talk to you a little bit about that.
So, um, the, this is a very scrappy job.
Yeah.
So you, you, like, mentioned that freelance is very scrappy.
So, like, this is a very scrappy job.
We don't, like, I'm trying to put processes in place, but we, we're just putting onboarding in place.
So there is a lot of, like, scrappiness that, that you need to just, like, sometimes you just need to grit your teeth and figure it out.
And, like, yeah, do it whatever.
Oh, and to be frank, I was.
It's actually kind of talking about like money and invoicing and chasing people like that.

Like I'm used to scrappy things, but yeah, I was trying to find a polite way to be like, it's inconsistent, you know, and people are like, oh, I didn't know you wanted to be paid every month and, you know, finding new clients and clients abruptly stopping that part, the chase kind of is what, what really winds me down.
I don't mind scrappiness.
I really like figuring out puzzles, figuring out processes.
Yeah, wanted, yeah, I just wanted to clarify that part.
Okay.
No, for sure.
For sure.
I'm glad you didn't.
Yeah.
I'm glad you wanted.
I was trying to find a polite way to be like, I'd just really like to know what I'm doing and, you know, know I'm going to have consistent work.
That'd be great.
Totally.
Okay, cool.
Well, we have 10 minutes left.
So what questions do have for me?
So you said you have like a number of MEs.
What do you think, like, is there like a certain skill set or personality thing or something that you think like makes a really successful?
don't would see a to I
Like, managing editor in these pods, like, that you see, like, in common among them?
Yeah, I mean, so there's, it's a tricky role because there are three things that really make you good at this role.
The first is, like, clear editorial strength, right?
Like, you have to be able to know what good looks like, and if the workflow spits out something that's bad, be able to say, like, this is bad.
And I really know why.
So that's kind of, like, one B, is, like, know why and know, like, be able to express what's bad and how to make it better.
So I think those are, like, two parts of the same coin in terms of editorial strength and skill set.
And then the other one is project management.
Some people just want to do editorial, but there are a lot of moving pieces.
I think we're growing the work streams that we're offering.
Like, right now, it's just, like, editorial and PSEO.
But, yeah.
Yeah.
And the, you know, long term, we're going to be offering more.
And so they're with our bigger clients, you know, we do have three or four work streams that are live.
That's cool.
Yeah.
Really cool.
Yeah, I mean, you mentioned scrappiness.
I know it's a young company.
What do you think, like, is the emerging, like, kind of culture, like, how does it feel over there?
Does it feel like, like, there's a consistent, like, energy or what?
Yeah, mean, I think that there's a, like, before and after of, like, we got a lot of clients all at once.
And some people were here in the before times when it was, like, incredibly scrappy.
And you kind of had to, it was, like, the Wild West.
And I think some people missed the Wild West a little bit.
But we're trying to create, like, some predictability.
And, yeah, predictability is really the thing where you can, like, anticipate how much work you're

We're to have, and what's going to work, and what's not, and how long something will take you, so some people are dealing with that better than others, and our platform is brand new, so we also, like, we used to run pieces, like, run a lot of this stuff directly in ChachiPT or Claude, we actually love Claude, we're Claude.
Yeah, that's awesome.

So we, so, like, some people still, like, want to just work directly in the AI, but because we're developing the platform, like, there has to be some appetite, I think, in a successful ME to, like, want to also play with the tech itself, and, like, a new tool, rather than just, like, the AI that they know.
For sure.
Yeah.
Yeah, and it is weird that we already have, like, yeah, that people are a little, like, team, yeah, exactly, but, like, but I know this window so well.
Yeah.
That's interesting.
Very cool.
And then, um, let's see.
What do you think?
Is there like any common reasons why like, I'm trying to think how to phrase it?

Like, is there any like, things that are consistently like, like, if something's going to go wrong, it's with this workflow or with this client expectation, like that you're working through right now?
Yeah.
Like, repeatable bumps?

I think it's, a lot of it is taking, we have a lot of artifacts is what we call them, but like informing pieces of content that, you know, like for ChatGPT and Claude, you can like add docs and files and things and like, and say like, this is how I want you to talk.
Like, this is a good example.
This is like, you know, everything that you could possibly know about the product.
Like, you know, write an article, but like, don't forget to, to reference these things.
I think
Workflows, like, sometimes they're like, yeah, I only did two or three of those.
Yeah, yeah.
That's so interesting.
did the exact same thing.
Yeah, really, like, thinking through every piece that you want it to take in.
But we actually, like, the tool now has, like, an AI, like, feedback panel where you can say, like, that wasn't exactly what I wanted.
Try again.
Oh, interesting.
Or, like, you didn't, you totally ignored this messaging doc or whatever.
exactly.
Very cool.
So, yeah, the tool is really exciting and cool to play with.
That's awesome.
Neat.
Well, I think that's about it from my side.
Just, yeah.
Awesome.
Cool.
Well, thank you so much for your time and for rescheduling again.
Sorry I missed you on the day.
Of But I, yeah, I'll, I think your next step is an exercise and possibly to talk to Panzer.
Panzer's much more technical.
He'll talk to you about workflows, and maybe show you Atlas, I don't know, but yeah.
Sounds fun.
Cool.
Awesome.
Thanks Felix, have a good one.
Thank you, you too, bye-bye.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Hiring manager interview with Felix Morgan for Technical Managing Editor role at GrowthX](https://fathom.video/share/CghgUgMDU-6NtY4kq3JfbYAUWAAr_m13?tab=summary&timestamp=66.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [GrowthX uses AI-powered content generation in a pod structure, with MEs managing 3-5 accounts](https://fathom.video/share/CghgUgMDU-6NtY4kq3JfbYAUWAAr_m13?tab=summary&timestamp=194.0)
  - [Felix has extensive B2B SaaS content experience and is enthusiastic about AI content generation](https://fathom.video/share/CghgUgMDU-6NtY4kq3JfbYAUWAAr_m13?tab=summary&timestamp=559.0)
  - [Role requires strong editorial skills, project management, and adaptability to new tech/processes](https://fathom.video/share/CghgUgMDU-6NtY4kq3JfbYAUWAAr_m13?tab=summary&timestamp=273.0)
  - [GrowthX is rapidly growing, onboarding 1-3 new clients weekly, with a developing internal platform](https://fathom.video/share/CghgUgMDU-6NtY4kq3JfbYAUWAAr_m13?tab=summary&timestamp=483.0)

## Topics

### GrowthX Structure and Role Overview

  - [Customer operations focus on process optimization and efficient deliverables](https://fathom.video/share/CghgUgMDU-6NtY4kq3JfbYAUWAAr_m13?tab=summary&timestamp=170.0)
  - [8-week strategy sprint followed by pod formation for ongoing client work](https://fathom.video/share/CghgUgMDU-6NtY4kq3JfbYAUWAAr_m13?tab=summary&timestamp=206.0)
  - [MEs manage 3-5 accounts, working with 1-2 content managers](https://fathom.video/share/CghgUgMDU-6NtY4kq3JfbYAUWAAr_m13?tab=summary&timestamp=227.0)
  - [In-house AI platform pulls from various LLMs for content generation](https://fathom.video/share/CghgUgMDU-6NtY4kq3JfbYAUWAAr_m13?tab=summary&timestamp=319.0)
  - [MEs edit, project manage, and provide feedback on AI workflows](https://fathom.video/share/CghgUgMDU-6NtY4kq3JfbYAUWAAr_m13?tab=summary&timestamp=346.0)
  - [Currently have 11 MEs, with 2 more joining soon; 20+ content managers](https://fathom.video/share/CghgUgMDU-6NtY4kq3JfbYAUWAAr_m13?tab=summary&timestamp=458.0)
  - [Onboarding 1-3 new clients weekly](https://fathom.video/share/CghgUgMDU-6NtY4kq3JfbYAUWAAr_m13?tab=summary&timestamp=483.0)

### Client Base and Services

  - [Primarily B2B SaaS clients across various industries (EdTech, architecture, manufacturing)](https://fathom.video/share/CghgUgMDU-6NtY4kq3JfbYAUWAAr_m13?tab=summary&timestamp=518.0)
  - [8-week strategy sprint followed by ongoing content production](https://fathom.video/share/CghgUgMDU-6NtY4kq3JfbYAUWAAr_m13?tab=summary&timestamp=206.0)
  - [Current focus on editorial and SEO, with plans to expand service offerings](https://fathom.video/share/CghgUgMDU-6NtY4kq3JfbYAUWAAr_m13?tab=summary&timestamp=426.0)

### Felix's Background and Experience

  - [Nearly a decade in copywriting, content strategy, and brand/creative work](https://fathom.video/share/CghgUgMDU-6NtY4kq3JfbYAUWAAr_m13?tab=summary&timestamp=559.0)
  - [Extensive B2B SaaS experience, both in-house and agency](https://fathom.video/share/CghgUgMDU-6NtY4kq3JfbYAUWAAr_m13?tab=summary&timestamp=570.0)
  - [Strong interest and experience in AI content generation](https://fathom.video/share/CghgUgMDU-6NtY4kq3JfbYAUWAAr_m13?tab=summary&timestamp=592.0)
  - [Currently freelancing, setting up AI content processes for clients](https://fathom.video/share/CghgUgMDU-6NtY4kq3JfbYAUWAAr_m13?tab=summary&timestamp=616.0)
  - [Comfortable with client-facing work and managing teams](https://fathom.video/share/CghgUgMDU-6NtY4kq3JfbYAUWAAr_m13?tab=summary&timestamp=685.0)

### AI Content Generation Insights

  - [Felix surprised by slow adoption of AI in content spaces, especially in tech](https://fathom.video/share/CghgUgMDU-6NtY4kq3JfbYAUWAAr_m13?tab=summary&timestamp=600.0)
  - [Uses ChatGPT, Gemini, and occasionally Claude for various content tasks](https://fathom.video/share/CghgUgMDU-6NtY4kq3JfbYAUWAAr_m13?tab=summary&timestamp=864.0)
  - [Emphasis on providing proper context and materials for AI to generate quality content](https://fathom.video/share/CghgUgMDU-6NtY4kq3JfbYAUWAAr_m13?tab=summary&timestamp=748.0)
  - [Experience with high-volume content production (e.g., 50 blogs, website relaunches)](https://fathom.video/share/CghgUgMDU-6NtY4kq3JfbYAUWAAr_m13?tab=summary&timestamp=718.0)

### GrowthX Culture and Challenges

  - [Transitioning from "Wild West" startup phase to more structured processes](https://fathom.video/share/CghgUgMDU-6NtY4kq3JfbYAUWAAr_m13?tab=summary&timestamp=1289.0)
